title: Main Street’s Pain, Wall Street’s Gain
abstract: We propose a fiscal policy expectations mechanism. When bad macro news arrives (in our study, when initial jobless claims (IJC) are higher than expected), investors may expect more generous government spending and drive up aggregate stock prices through the expected cash flow channel. Using a time-series sample from January 2013 to March 2021, we find that this phenomenon emerges when newspapers mention fiscal policy more. In the cross section, firms expected to receive more government spending – through stimulus supports during COVID-19 or procurement contracts before 2020 – exhibit higher individual stock returns when bad IJC shocks arrive.

author.Nancy R. Xu*: Seidner Department of Finance, Carroll School of Management, Boston College, United States of America
author.Yang You: Faculty of Business and Economics, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong

article.1. Introduction: While actual fiscal policy bills get passed relatively infrequently, people’s beliefs about the prospect of bills passing can change on a dailybasis. However, there is little empirical research on the role of changing expectations about fiscal policy in the stock market.2 In this paper, we propose a “fiscal policy expectations” mechanism in the effect of macro announcement surprises on stock returns. In a low-interest-rate, crisis environment, when Main Street suffers more than expected, investors may expect more generous federal government spending through fiscal policy (FP), driving up expected future cash flow growth and stock prices.Given the lack of futures markets or surveys to elicit high-frequency FP expectations, we provide evidence for two testable predictions of this mechanism and focus on the weekly initial jobless claims (IJC) announcements as the main macro events in the paper. The first prediction is that return responses to IJC shocks should be higher during periods with more expansionary FP expectations, particularly when bad IJC news arrives. In a time-series sample from January 2013 to March 2021, we construct measures based on news articles on IJC announcement days to capture time-varying expectations of FP. The second prediction is that when FP is actively discussed nationwide amid heightened budgetary debates, firms and industries that are expected to receive more fiscal spending should exhibit higher individual stock returns when bad IJC shocks appear. We first use a specific Covid period (early 2020 to March 2021) with unprecedented fiscal activities as our identification strategy, and construct three cross sections based on a granular Covid stimulus database, actual stimulus bill textual analysis, and firm fundamentals. In addition, we generalize the cross-sectional test to our pre-2020 sample, where the dominant firm-level government spending is in the form of procurement contracts.Initial Jobless Claims (IJC) are announced every Thursday at 8:30 a.m. Eastern Time, and IJC surprises or shocks in our paper are defined as the percent difference between actual and expected IJC numbers. The higher the shocks are, the worse the news is. We first highlight two stylized facts from IJC announcement event days. For one, news articles that are written to describe IJC announcement news on the same day discuss different topics (such as fiscal policy (FP), monetary policy (MP), and uncertainty) at different points during our sample period. There are fluctuations in FP-related mentions before 2019, typically at the height of government shutdown concerns and budgetary debates; moreover, FP mentions jump 2–3 standard deviations (SD) higher after 2020, particularly when solely arising from bad IJC announcements. These heightened FP mentions typically come with expansionary speculation, as the quote on Page 1 shows. Second, in a subsample from February 2020 to March 2021 with unprecedented FP mentions, stock returns significantly rise with IJC shocks, overturning the “bad is bad” pricing from paradigm models. A one SD increase in the IJC surprise leads to significant increases in daily open-to-close market index returns of around 30 basis points. The phenomenon affects the market through the expected cash flow growth channel and only exists during bad IJC days. These stylized facts motivate our fiscal policy expectations hypothesis.To test the time-series prediction, we interact IJC shocks with time-varying topic mentions of FP, MP, and uncertainty. In the 2013–2021 sample, when FP mentions are one SD higher than average, stock return responses caused by a 0.1 unit increase in bad IJC shocks are 24–30 basis points higher. This finding is robust to controlling for monetary policy and uncertainty mentions. We next find that return responses to good IJC shocks are not associated with fiscal policy expectations but with monetary policy expectations (gauged by either text- or survey-based measures). This finding is consistent with predictions in Boyd et al. (2005) and empirical results in Elenev et al. (2024). In a low-interest-rate environment (which is what our sample focuses on), interest rates should have more potential to increase, and good IJC shocks should be able to trigger more expectations in such increases.We next introduce our two cross section empirical strategies, in Covid and pre-Covid samples, respectively. First, we use the period with active fiscal stimulus debates from February 2020 to March 2021 to our identification advantage. During this period, fiscal stimulus bills received unprecedented public attention and should be economically relevant to almost all industries and firms. To capture industry- or firm-level fiscal policy expectations, we construct three cross sections based on various textual or archival data sources. Investors may infer the likelihood of a particular industry receiving more fiscal support from reading industry mentions in actual stimulus bills. We search industry mentions in the major stimulus bills using industry keywords from the NAICS website. Industries mentioned more heavily in actual bills exhibit statistically higher return-IJC shock correlations, supporting our hypothesis. For instance, the healthcare industry receives a considerable amount of fiscal subsidy, given the nature of the pandemic crisis, and demonstrates a high industry return-IJC shock correlation at 0.228. Several non-crisis-related industries (e.g., transportation, manufacturing) with more mentions in the actual bills also exhibit a stronger “Main Street pain, Wall Street gain” phenomenon.We next construct a novel database based on raw data from a Treasury Office website (USAspending.gov) that reports all promised and actual fiscal distributions from the Federal government to each firm under the legal bills. Fiscal distributions under the Covid stimulus bills (CARES of 3/27/2020, CAA of 12/27/2020, and ARP of 3/11/2021) are largely in the form of a Paycheck Protection Program (PPP) or other forgivable loans. We find that firms that are promised greater stimulus funding exhibit statistically higher return-IJC shock correlations and sensitivities. Importantly, we also use only promised PPP distributions (which should have a direct connection with labor market outcomes) and find both economically and statistically similar results. Consistent with our legal bill textual analysis study, the healthcare and air transportation industries receive the greatest stimulus support during the early pandemic.Last but not least, we obtain changes in firm fundamentals from 2019 to early 2020 to quantify the initial effect of Covid on these firms; this should signal the fiscal support a firm is expected to receive from the Federal government. These fundamental measures include job postings, employment, revenues, and earnings per share. Results remain robust at the 5% significance level.In our second empirical strategy to test the cross-section prediction, we generalize it to our pre-Covid sample from 2013 to 2019, where fiscal spending to firms is primarily in the form of government procurement contracts, as confirmed both in terms of absolute dollar amounts and public attention. Specifically, we construct a detailed Government Procurement dataset based on USAspending.gov, and use the recent past procurement obligation amounts (unscaled or scaled by recent revenue) as proxies for firm- and industry-level future fiscal dependence or sensitivity. Our cross-sectional results remain robust, both statistically and economically. During times with relatively heightened fiscal discussions, firms with more dependence on fiscal spending exhibit significantly higher stock returns in response to a unit of bad IJC shocks. Before 2020, such times align with heightened government shutdown concerns and budgetary debates (such as early 2013 and 2019) according to our textual analysis. Our procurement-based fiscal dependence measure provides a novel and more granular alternative to the U.S. National Income and Product Accounts (NIPA)-based measure as used in Belo et al. (2013).
article.Related literature: Our research contributes to the economics and finance literature in several ways. First, we join the literature that shows that macro announcements matter to the stock market (e.g., Gilbert, 2011, Savor and Wilson, 2013, Ai and Bansal, 2018, Hirshleifer and Sheng, 2021, Fisher et al., 2022, among many others). In particular, our work joins existing papers that study the time series pattern of stock market reactions to macro announcement surprises. The literature typically settles on two explanations. There is a business-cycle explanation (e.g., McQueen and Roley, 1993, Andersen et al., 2007) that predicts that business conditions reinforce the pricing of macro shocks during contractionary times. Another strand (e.g., Boyd et al., 2005, Elenev et al., 2024, Yang and Zhu, 2021, Caballero and Simsek, 2021) argues that time-varying return responses to macro news likely also depend on monetary policy expectations, which are often but not always correlated with business cycles.3 Our research contributes to this literature by providing empirical evidence of the existence and economic significance of a specific cash flow expectation mechanism, i.e., time-varying fiscal spending expectations.Next, our empirical evidence also complements the existing fiscal policy literature. While there is an extensive economics literature on the macroeconomic effects of fiscal policy,4 there is scant research on its asset pricing effects. Most studies (see Croce et al., 2012a, Croce et al., 2012b, Gomes et al., 2013, Bretscher et al., 2020, Croce et al., 2021, etc.) focus on examining the long-term price effects of actual policies (tax and spending) at quarterly or lower frequencies though the lens of an equilibrium framework. Meanwhile, empirical work by Belo et al. (2013) and Belo and Yu (2013) examines the impact of actual government spending and tax policies on the cross section of equity risk premia, and Diercks and Waller (2017) and Elenev et al. (2022) examine the interplay between monetary policy, fiscal policy, and risk premia. More related to our work, Bianchi et al. (2021) document the role of congressional tweets in changing investor expectations about a bill passing. Xu et al. (2024) show that analysts form fiscal risk perceptions of procurement contracts. Theoretical work, including studies by Christiano et al. (2011) and Bianchi et al. (2023), has also predicted that fiscal policy would result in a much stronger effect to the real economy when monetary policy is less active. Our research contributes to this literature by hypothesizing that fiscal policy already affects the capital market by affecting investor expectations, which get capitalized at a high frequency. We use labor news announcements as an identification strategy to help “sign” investor fiscal policy expectations, and a low-interest-rate sample period helps constrain MP behaviors on the lax policy side.In a broader view, our paper documents an expected “Government Put” among investors, as fiscal policy and particularly spending policies have recently become more active and sizable. We plan to continue updating the new fiscal-related datasets used in our paper, such as the news-based FP mention measures and cross-sectional fiscal distributions, to make them available to other researchers.The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 establishes stylized facts about newspaper topics and asset price responses on IJC announcement days; together, they motivate the general mechanism hypothesized in Section 3. Sections 4, 5, and 6 test the time-series and cross-sectional predictions. Section 7 presents conceptual and extending empirical discussions. Section 8 offers concluding remarks.
article.2. Stylized facts: In this section, we establish several stylized facts about newspaper topic discussions and asset price responses on initial jobless claims (IJC) announcement days in the recent decade. We use initial jobless claims as our primary macro announcement shock for several reasons. First, among various macro announcements in the U.S., only IJC announcements occur at a weekly frequency (08:30 a.m. Eastern Time every Thursday), and such timely releases generate more observations. Second, real-time jobless numbers are unarguably “Main Street” variables, and should matter to policymakers.5Our main IJC shock is defined as IJCShockt=IJCt−Et−Δ(IJCt)Et−Δ(IJCt), where IJCt denotes the number of actual initial claims from the prior week announced during this week, t, by the Employment and Training Administration (ETA), and Et−Δ(IJCt) indicates the median survey forecasts submitted before the announcement time. Both actual and expected claims are obtained from Bloomberg. We consider only IJC announcement days that neither overlap with Federal Open Market Committee meetings (FOMC) or other major macro announcements nor exhibit statistically outlying behaviors.62.1. Textual analysis on IJC announcement daysWe examine topic mentions on IJC announcement days. We use CNBC’s IJC news articles, which are written each Thursday to describe and interpret that morning’s IJC announcement. Unlike other news sources such as WSJ or Bloomberg, CNBC has a designated website for Initial Jobless Claims announcements, https://www.cnbc.com/jobless-claims/. A team of CNBC reporters writes and publishes one article about each Thursday’s IJC announcement by noon that day; a handful of times they collaborate with reporters from Reuters. We manually collect these CNBC IJC news articles on announcement days as far back as is available.7 We are able to identify 366 IJC articles from the CNBC website through March 18, 2021, when we began collecting data for this project. Fig. 1 shows the article distribution over time. We obtain only a few articles from before 2013 on their website, while the article collection becomes quite stable afterwards. This limits the start year of our textual analysis to 2013. The bottom plot shows a relatively stable split between bad and good IJC announcements per 60-announcement rolling window.We construct time-varying topic mentions metrics of 5 general topics: Fiscal policy (“FP”), monetary policy (“MP”), economic uncertainty (“UNC”), coronavirus-related (“COVID”), and normal words that appear in describing IJC (“NORMAL”). We relegate detailed lists of keywords and empirical methodology to Online Appendix section OB and discuss the key points below. First, general textbook terms that define fiscal policy, such as “fiscal policy”, “tax”, or “government debt”, are not typically how fiscal policy as a topic gets mentioned in labor news announcement articles. To accommodate the needs of our research, we developed a group of words that reflect discussions of government spending, grants to the states, transfers (augmented unemployment benefits), and lawmaking to capture fiscal policy mentions. For instance, when words and phrases such as “aid”, “extend”, “benefit”, “Congress”, “lawmaker”, and “federal government” appear in one article, that typically reflects an ongoing fiscal-related discussion.8 The second topic of interest is monetary policy. The word list includes “central bank”, “inflation”, “Federal Reserve”, Federal Reserve Chairpersons’ last names, and so on. The third topic is economic uncertainty, and we follow Baker et al. (2016)’s word list. The last two topics – Covid-related and normal IJC terms – are included for validation purposes. Finally, we use state-of-the-art “Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency” or “TF-IDF” scores in our textual analysis to obtain the topic mentions metrics (see a recent survey in Beel et al., 2016). In general, the score of a word (after stemming and lemmatization) increases proportionally to the number of times this word appears in the document (Luhn, 1957). In our research, the average of the TF-IDF scores of all words in the same topic then becomes the topic’s score. Given that each IJC article is relatively short (average = 327 words), we always construct topic mentions metrics using groups of weeks.Download: Download high-res image (689KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Summary of CNBC jobless claim articles on IJC announcement days.The data collection process is described in Section 2.1. Top plot: number of articles we are able to scrape from the CNBC website each year. Bottom plot: in a rolling 60-week window (time stamp = last day of the rolling window), the fraction of articles with bad (good) IJC surprises is shown in the top blue (bottom red) area. The last 60-week rolling window is from 20200130 to 20210318 (end of the paper sample). Other details can be found in Online Appendix OB. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)https://www.cnbc.com/jobless-claims/.To illustrate, Fig. 2 shows time-varying topic mentions using 60-week rolling windows, scaled by the “Normal-IJC” mentions from the same rolling window. As a result, levels are comparable. The relative mentions of economic uncertainty (see the blue dotted line) exhibit patterns similar to those generally documented in the existing literature (such as Jurado et al., 2015, Baker et al., 2016, Bekaert et al., 2022). The pattern peaks locally around the Brexit referendum in 2016, the China-U.S. trade war in 2018–2019, the initial Covid wave in early 2020, and then the U.S. election in late 2020.Download: Download high-res image (417KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. What do people talk about on IJC announcement days?This figure shows the topic mentions in rolling 60-week windows of news articles released on IJC announcement days. The four topic mentions are scaled by mentions of normal IJC words (see Section 2.1 for more details). For instance, 0.2 in the y-axis means that this topic’s keywords are mentioned 20 times per 100 normal IJC words. The timestamp refers to the last day of the rolling window.Next, the two policy mentions – fiscal (black solid) and monetary (red dashed) – show distinctive patterns. Fiscal policy was heavily discussed during the 2013–2014 government shutdown debates, and our FP mentions variable indeed captures that early in our sample (2013–2014). Anticipations about FP increased during the extremely active Atlantic hurricane season in late 2017-early 2018 that caused an estimated $295 billion in damage in businesses and households (source: Wikipedia.org), and increased again in early 2019 with another government shutdown discussion. During the Covid crisis, mentions of fiscal policy did not sharply increase until April 2020 and continued to rise in the rest of the sample. This ongoing increase since April 2020 brings FP mentions to an unprecedented level. From the beginning to the end of the sample, FP mentions increase by 57% (p-value = 0.002) and significantly surpass MP mentions. MP mentions on IJC announcement days visibly increased from late 2016 to late 2018 and then declined, with another bump in early 2020; the level of MP mentions ends 49.0% lower than it was at the beginning of the sample (p-value = 0.000). It is noteworthy that MP should have been well attended to during 2020–2021, especially before April 2020, as most of the conventional and unconventional polices were announced before that month;9 however, our textual analysis focuses on what CNBC discusses when IJC news arrive, which helps with empirical identification. Fig. 3 examines topic mention asymmetries. We construct “bad” (“good”) topic mentions variables using articles on bad (good) IJC days within the same 60-week rolling window. In this figure, we scale a topic’s bad or good day mentions using its first value in the sample, as we are interested in its relative growth; that is, “1.5” in the y-axis means that topic mentions increase by 50% compared to the beginning of the sample. All these point estimates exhibit narrow 95% confidence intervals (see Online Appendix Figure OB1).Download: Download high-res image (586KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 3. What do people talk about on bad and good IJC announcement days?This figure complements Fig. 2 and shows the relative topic mentions on bad (thick lines) and good (thin lines) IJC days within the same 60-week rolling window. For interpretation purposes, each line is scaled with the first value in its series. For instance, 1.5 in the y-axis means that mentions of this topic are 50% higher than at the beginning of the sample. The timestamp always refers to the last day of the rolling window.The upper left plot of Fig. 3 shows that FP mentions grow more aggressively on bad IJC days, and are mainly responsible for explaining the FP pattern from Fig. 2. During this sample period, the growth of FP mentions on bad IJC days is on average significantly higher than on good IJC days (p-value = 0.011), indicating that FP speculations often change when bad IJC news arrives and hence can be interpreted as expansionary FP expectations. On the other hand, the pattern of MP mentions on good IJC days more aligns with the overall MP pattern, also exhibiting a clear hump from late 2016 to late 2018 relative to 2015. Therefore, higher MP mentions on good IJC days can be interpreted as more contractionary MP expectations, which can be validated by their significant and positive correlation (0.46) with the quarterly revisions in future interest rate expectations (source: Survey of Professional Forecasters).10Taken together, we find that IJC news is associated with different topics at different times. What is new and interesting is that speculations about fiscal policy, particularly when bad IJC news arrives, appear sizable, exhibiting intuitive time variations.2.2. Asset price responses on IJC announcement daysWe next study the responses of stock returns and discount-rate-sensitive variables (denoted by yt) to IJC shocks (denoted by IJCShockt) on announcement event days: (1)yt=β0+β1IJCShockt+ɛt.To streamline the results, we compare two zero-lower-bound periods with different fiscal activities: July 2009 to December 2015, labeled “Normal”, and then February 2020 to March 2021, labeled “Covid”. Table 1 demonstrates the unusual stock return behaviors during February 2020 to March 2021 and shows that these changes are unlikely explained through discount rate channels.Columns (1) and (2) use the daily open-to-close log S&P 500 and Dow Jones 65 returns (unit: basis points; source: DataStream) as the dependent variables, respectively. During the normal period, daily open-to-close market returns exhibit a negative (insignificant) response to IJC shocks. During the Covid period, market returns increase significantly by around 31 basis points with a 10% IJC shock. In economic magnitude, a one SD increase in the IJC shock leads to a 0.2 SD increase in daily open-to-close market returns. The literature typically finds that changes in futures prices around the event time exhibit more significant responses than daily returns, and we confirm this in our normal period using 5-min E-mini S&P500 and Dow futures data (see Table A.2, Table A.3). Moreover, we also obtain the similar Covid period result as in Table 1’s Panel B, both statistically and economically. We label this headline observation the “Main Street pain, Wall Street gain” phenomenon — bad labor news drives up stock prices.Table 1. Stylized facts: The pricing channel.We next examine the responses of various discount-rate-sensitive asset variables. During the normal period, when the IJC shock increases, we mainly observe that long-term Treasury yields decrease significantly (see column (3) of Table 1). As shown in columns (4) and (5), both the level and implied volatility of the short rate do not respond to IJC shocks, which is expected given the zero lower bound. Yield changes mostly come from changes in inflation expectations. We use Treasury yield and TIPS series to construct proxies for daily changes in 10-year inflation expectations and find that one SD increase in IJC shocks also leads to a 0.12 SD decrease in inflation expectations given the weaker growth prospect.11 In contrast, during the Covid period, when the IJC shock increases, both changes in long-term yields and inflation expectations do not respond as before, yet stock returns increase significantly (column (1)). In fact, Panel C shows that coefficients for stock returns and changes in inflation expectations are significantly higher during the Covid period. These observations suggest that the counteracting force likely works through the expected growth channel.Of course, one alternative hypothesis to explain stock responses during the Covid period is that worse-than-expected labor news could trigger an expectation of a longer ZLB period, and stock returns increase as a result of that expectation. We directly test this hypothesis by constructing several path variables (i.e., the difference between n- and 1-month ahead Fed Funds futures (FFF) rates) and would expect a negative coefficient under this alternative hypothesis. According to column group (6) of Table 1, we find statistically similar evidence between these two periods, and importantly, we do not find the Covid-period coefficient to be negative.To gain more insights, we zero in on the Covid-period stock returns result by considering bad and good IJC days separately and by directly studying earnings growth forecast responses. The first plot of Fig. 4 illustrates that the positive β1 in Table 1’s column (1) mainly comes from days when the actual IJC numbers are higher than expected (i.e., bad IJC days). In fact, the pattern is quite robust using other headline indices, especially the Dow Jones indices. On days when actual IJC numbers are lower than expected, β1 is visually negative for all indices except for Nasdaq 100.Download: Download high-res image (1022KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 4. Relation between stock variable responses and IJC shocks from February 2020 to March 2021.This figure demonstrates the data points that produce Table 2. Table 2 formalizes this figure and confirms that the positive relation between returns and IJC shocks is statistically significant only when we consider bad IJC days (see Panel A). R2s during bad IJC days are meaningfully higher than those during good days, with a difference of around 15%. A one SD increase in IJC shocks corresponds to a 0.4 SD increase in S&P500 and Dow Jones returns. Using changes in daily value-weighted U.S. earnings growth forecasts (source: the I/B/E/S database & the authors’ calculation) as a dependent variable, we find that changes in earnings growth expectations increase significantly with IJC shocks only on bad IJC days, as shown in column (7) of Table 2. The economic magnitude is 0.411 SD per SD shock, which is also similar to the total stock returns responses from columns (1) and (2). The last plot of Fig. 4 similarly illustrates the raw data. This evidence directly indicates that the underlying mechanism could affect stock returns through changing cash flow expectations, which is also consistent with the observation that Dow Jones firms, which are more cash flow sensitive, respond more than Nasdaq firms in our sample.Table 2. Stylized facts: Asymmetry in stock variables.
article.3. Mechanism hypothesis: The stylized facts from Section 2 motivate a specific cash flow mechanism for macro shocks to affect stock returns. In this section, we generalize these stylized facts conceptually and present testable hypotheses for the rest of the paper.We propose a fiscal policy expectations mechanism. In a low-interest-rate crisis environment, when Main Street suffers more than expected, investors may expect more generous federal government spending through fiscal policy, driving up expected future cash flow growth and stock prices. We expect this mechanism to be time-varying, as our textual analysis already shows that perceptions of policy tools do change over time (see Fig. 2). During periods when discussions about fiscal policy and related debates are not heightened, the “Main Street pain, Wall Street gain” pattern in stock returns should diminish.It is however empirically challenging to test this mechanism, given that there are no readily observable futures markets or high-frequency surveys to elicit investor FP expectations. As a result, we examine two testable predictions:1.Time series: Time-varying FP expectations, as a state variable, should explain time-varying return responses to IJC shocks, particularly on bad IJC days.2.Cross section:When FP is actively discussed nationwide, firms and industries that are expected to receive more fiscal support or spending should exhibit higher individual stock returns when bad IJC shocks occur.To test the first prediction (see Section 4), we proxy time-varying FP expectations from 2013 to 2021 using news-based measures mentioned earlier. The framework also flexibly controls for other aforementioned mechanisms such as monetary policy. To test the second prediction (see Sections 5 Mechanism: Cross-sectional evidence during covid, 6 Mechanism: Pre-covid evidence using government procurement award data), we present two pieces of evidence. We first use the period with active Covid Stimulus Package debates from 02/2020 to 03/2021 to our identification advantage and construct three detailed firm- and industry-level cross sections based on various textual or archival data sources (the stimulus bill texts, the Treasury registry office, and firm fundamentals). Second, given the lack of widely-distributed stimulus data before 2020, we generalize the cross-sectional test to our pre-Covid sample from 2013 to 2019 and use detailed government procurement data to proxy for firm-time fiscal dependence. It is worth highlighting that some of these cross-sectional datasets that we construct are being used for the first time in the asset pricing literature. We relegate more theoretical discussions to Section 7.
article.4. Mechanism: Time-series evidence: We span the time variation in return sensitivities to IJC shocks on IJC announcement days with several state variables that are of interest. The specification is as follows: (2)yt=β0+β1IJCshockt+β2Zτ+β3IJCshockt∗Zτ+ɛt,where t and τ denote day and quarter, respectively; y is the daily market returns (in basis points) on IJC announcement days; Z is one or multiple quarterly state variable(s), which is standardized for interpretation purpose. The unit of observation is the IJC announcement event day, and we exclude overlaps with macro and monetary announcement dates as before. As a result, the sample period is January 2013 to March 2021, and we consider a bad IJC sample (N = 184) and a good IJC sample (N = 189). To obtain quarterly state variables for the bad IJC (good IJC) sample, we use all news articles that correspond to bad (good) IJC announcement days within a specific quarter to construct the bad (good) topic-mention scores (see construction details of the TF-IDF scores in Section 2.1). Three quarterly state variables are fiscal policy (FP), monetary policy (MP), and uncertainty (UNC). In addition, we follow Elenev et al. (2024) and also consider the difference between the one-quarter-ahead forecast and the nowcast of the 3-month Treasury bill rate (“ΔTbill3m”, Tbill3mτ+1|τ−1−Tbill3mτ|τ−1; source: Survey of Professional Forecasters (SPF)); this variable captures revisions in the future interest rate, conceptually close to the text-based MP measures.As discussed in Section 2.1, higher FP (MP) mentions during this sample period (2013–2021) can be generally interpreted as more expansionary (contractionary) policy expectations. Good IJC day MP mentions and ΔTbill3m are also significantly and positively correlated at 0.46 (p-value = 0.000). One disadvantage of the SPF-based quarterly interest rate forecast data is that we do not know when the survey was conducted, on good IJC, bad IJC, or non-macro-announcement days, whereas for our text-based measures we do know when the texts were written. Finally, the quarterly FP and MP mentions are statistically uncorrelated during the sample period, regardless of the bad or good IJC day sample.Table 3 reports the regression results of Eq. (2), where the interaction coefficients are of interest. First, columns (1) and (2) are our main result using open-to-close S&P500 log returns and DowJones65 log returns, respectively. On bad IJC announcement days, when fiscal policy mentions are one SD higher than average, stock return responses to a 10% IJC shock are 28 basis points higher, given the significant and positive interaction estimates. Both columns control for the other competing channel mentioned earlier (MP expectations). In columns (3) and (4), we see that MP mentions and rate forecast revisions (ΔTbill3m) play an insignificant role in explaining return responses to bad IJC shocks.Table 3. Fiscal policy (FP), monetary policy (MP), and uncertainty (UNC) are the topic mentions of interest; those used in Panel A (B) are constructed using all bad (good) IJC announcement day news articles from the same quarter. The textual analysis methodology is the same as described in Section 2.1. We also consider the difference between the one-quarter-ahead forecast and the nowcast of the 3-month Treasury bill rate (“ΔTbill3m”), where both forecast and nowcast are provided given the last quarter’s information set (source: Survey of Professional Forecasters, or SPF). Due to news article availability, our sample runs from January 2013 to March 2021 and excludes outlier, macro, and FOMC announcement days (as done throughout the paper). Robustness and other univariate regression results are shown in Tables A.4 and OA2. Robust standard errors are reported in parentheses; ***, p-value <1%; **, <5%; *, <10%.We conduct several robustness tests and show them in Table A.4. First, our main finding of the positive interaction coefficient with FP is both statistically and economically robust if we only include FP interaction terms (see columns (1) and (2)), which is not surprising because FP is not significantly correlated with other controls in our construction and sample period. We relegate other univariate results to Internet Appendix Table OA2. Second, columns (3)–(6) of Table A.4 complement columns (3) and (4) of Table 3 by incorporating ΔTbill3m and UNC into the model one at a time, and our results remain intact.Given that FP mentions are categorically higher during 2020–2021, we also examine whether our results exist from 01/2013 and 12/2019 (N = 154). As shown in columns (7)–(10) of Table A.4, we find that while the statistical significance of the interaction term decreases from 1% significance to 10% significance, the main qualitative and quantitative results remain robust. This is an indication that the FP state variable variations already have the ability to explain time-varying return responses to IJC shocks before 2020. What is different is that the rise in FP speculations during the Covid period is strong enough (i.e., around 2–3 SDs higher than the full sample average) to completely overturn the sign of return responses to IJC shocks, and as a result, a simple subsample regression yields significant and positive coefficients (see Table 1, Table 2). On the other hand, the pre-Covid FP effect shows in smaller-than-usual decreases in returns when bad IJC shocks arrive. Before 2020, increases in FP mentions when bad IJC news arrives can typically be attributed to periods around government shutdown threats. For instance, on January 10, 2013, actual IJC numbers were higher than expected, but the open-to-close stock returns ended up 16% higher; CNBC commented on this news with speculation that “Congress must vote to raise the government’s $16.4 trillion borrowing limit by around late February”. One can find similar textual evidence during early 2018 and early 2019 when discussions of and congressional debates about government shutdowns were heightened.We conclude this section by discussing good IJC day results, as presented in Panel B of Table 3. On the one hand, fiscal policy mentions do not explain the time-varying return responses, given insignificant interaction coefficients. On the other hand, when monetary policy mentions are one SD higher than average, stock return responses to a -10% IJC shock are 30 basis points lower. This evidence during our sample period is consistent with the existing evidence of monetary policy expectations (as in Boyd et al., 2005, Elenev et al., 2024). In column (8), we replace MP mentions with survey-based ΔTbill3m and find similar results. This is not surprising given the significant correlation between the two state variables as mentioned before.
article.5. Mechanism: Cross-sectional evidence d: In this section, we test the cross-section prediction from Section 3 in the Covid-period sample from February 2020 to March 2021, excluding outlier IJC shocks and overlaps with macro and monetary policy announcements as before. During this period, fiscal stimulus bills received unprecedented public attention and should reach almost all industries and firms, allowing us to observe enough cross-firm variation.When a bad IJC shock arrives, investors may expect an increase in the likelihood that an expansionary fiscal policy bill – in the case of the Covid period, a stimulus bill – will be passed. This change in belief could affect expectations about cash flow growth in the economy; firms and industries that are expected to receive more fiscal support thus exhibit higher individual stock returns. We construct three granular-level datasets. Under the prediction, higher return responses to IJC shocks should occur in:1.Industries that are mentioned more in the actual stimulus bills.2.Firms that are promised more fiscal funding by the federal government.3.Firms that are expected to suffer more from Covid-related impacts.Sections 5.1, 5.2, and 5.3 introduce each of the three cross-sectional measures and present evidence. To be consistent with the time series section, we primarily consider the S&P 500 universe. All cross-sectional tests robustly support our hypothesis.5.1. Industry mentions in actual stimulus billsInvestors may infer the likelihood of a particular firm/industry receiving more fiscal support than others from relative industry mentions in actual bills. In this cross section, we directly search for industry mentions in the four stimulus legal bills intended to address the Covid crisis. Three bills were actually signed into law (CARES of 3/27/2020, CAA of 12/27/2020, ARP of 3/11/2021); the Health and Economic Recovery Omnibus Emergency Solutions (HEROES) Act passed the House on May 15, 2020 and was also heavily debated in but did not pass the Senate 6 months later. In all cases, we use the final versions of these bills (source: Congress.gov) to conduct textual analysis. We count industry keywords for each 2-digit NAICS industry, where each industry keyword list comprises words from its respective 6-digit NAICS website. We consider one bill at a time, and focus on the 14 industries that have reasonable representations (≥5 firms) in the S&P500 firm universe.12 Fig. 5 illustrates a significant and positive relationship between industry mentions in the CARES Act on the x-axis and industry return correlations with IJC shocks on the y-axis. For interpretation purposes, we present correlation, which can also be interpreted as economic magnitude in SDs. Industry-level return-IJC correlations are the average of individual stock return-IJC correlations. The fitted line yields a significant and positive correlation coefficient of 0.44 (p-value = 0.067). Results using the other three stimulus bills hold (see Fig. A.2).Download: Download high-res image (485KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 5. Cross-sectional evidence: Industry keyword mentions in the CARES Act and industry returns-IJC correlations.This figure depicts the relationship between industry returns-IJC shock correlations and their mentions in the actual final Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security (CARES) Act. Construction of industry-level correlation (y-axis): we calculate the correlations between individual stock returns and the IJC shocks of the S&P 500 universe from February 2020 to March 2021. We drop shock outliers and major macro and monetary policy announcement dates. We use 2-digit NAICS codes to classify firms into industries and then calculate the industry-level average correlation. Six industries have fewer than 5 firms representing them among the S&P 500 stocks, and they are therefore excluded from this analysis. Construction of industry mentions in the actual bill (x-axis): We generate industry-level keyword lists from the 6-digit NAICS industry classification webpages. For instance, keywords for “21 Mining” are obtained from https://www.naics.com/six-digit-naics/?v=2017&code=21. Then, we identify mentions of this industry in the actual bills. The CARES Act: This bill was initially introduced in the House of Representatives on January 24, 2019 as H.R. 748, the Middle Class Health Benefits Tax Repeal Act of 2019; it passed the House on July 17, 2019. It then passed the Senate as the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act on March 25, 2020, and was signed into law by President Donald Trump on March 27, 2020. In Fig. A.2, we reproduce the same plot using the HEROES, CAA, and ARP Acts as robustness tests. The fitted line above has a significant and high correlation of 0.44 (SE = 0.24).Healthcare industries are among the most mentioned in the CARES Act, as might be expected given the nature of the pandemic crisis, with a high industry return-IJC shock correlation at 0.228 (p-value = 0.016). It is worth noting that there are other non-crisis-related industries with frequent mentions in the CARES Act that also exhibited high stock return-IJC shock correlation. One example is the transportation industry, with an industry return-IJC correlation of 0.186 (p-value = 0.092). At least three titles in the CARES Act (e.g., Titles II, VI, XII) and five sections in the ARP Act (e.g., Continued Assistance to Rail Workers, Public Transportation, Transportation and Infrastructure, and Aviation Manufacturing Jobs Protection) heavily mention transportation-related industries.This textual analysis is the first indication of the role of fiscal policy expectations in shaping cross-industry differences in return responses to IJC shocks. In the rest of Section 5, we explore more direct and granular (firm-level) fiscal support variables and formalize them in a panel regression framework.5.2. Promised and actual fiscal spending to firmsIn our second cross section, we construct from scratch a new dataset of all Covid-crisis-related stimulus spending to each firm (and its subsidiaries) based on public records on https://www.USAspending.gov/.13 Intuitively, investors would expect certain firms to receive more fiscal support if government documents indicate more amounts have been promised to these firms or if they have been distributed more emergency support in the past. In the context of Covid stimulus packages, the term “awards” in this dataset indicates a forgivable loan or grant, and this dataset provides both promised/obligated amounts and actual gross outlays at the award-time level. Such awards can be identified under a category named “Disaster Emergency Fund Codes”. In a clear subcategory (i.e., when Disaster Emergency Fund Codes = O or P), we are able to identify awards under the Paycheck Protection Program (PPP), a specific labor-related fiscal support. Online Appendix section OC provides further description of our data source and collection process.Two patterns of these awards are useful for our research. First, such awards are sizable, providing direct positive cash flows for these firms to mitigate Covid damage. Second and more importantly, current actual award amounts to a firm are indicative of what this firm is likely receiving in subsequent supports/bills. For instance, shortly after the Covid crisis began in the U.S., American Airlines received 6 billion dollars on April 21, 2020 as part of the payroll support program in the CARES Act, enacted on March 27, 2020. Then the company received another 3.3 billion dollars in obligated payroll support, authorized under Subtitle A of Title IV of the CAA, 2021.14 In the S&P 500 ticker list, we identify 138 companies that have ever received such emergency awards.15 Such awards at the firm level are skewed: out of the 138 companies, 108 companies received less than one million dollars, 24 companies received one million to one billion dollars, and 6 companies received more than one billion dollars. The healthcare and transportation industries were promised (and actually did receive) large amounts. For firms that we do not find stimulus spending matches for, we fill actual and promised support with zeros because a zero meaningfully indicates the lack of emergency awards for these firms. These statistical patterns also motivate that necessary numerical transformations (such as logarithms or with size or revenue scaling) to the raw total award data should be applied.We focus on three firm-level fiscal support variables: total obligated amount across all fiscal spending types during the Covid period, total obligated amount from the Paycheck Protection Program only, and total actual gross outlays. The second variable is our preferred main measure because the PPP should directly affect the labor market. Most PPP obligations are issued under the CARES act. Fig. 6 depicts the relationship between the log of PPP awards at the firm level and its return-IJC shock correlation. For illustration purposes, we sort firms into four groups by their obligated PPP awards: (i) Not a Covid funding recipient (log(1 + PPP award) = 0); (ii) log(1 + PPP award) from 0 to 10; (iii) log(1 + PPP award) from 10 to 15; (iv) log(1 + PPP award) above 15. The economic interpretation is quite monotonic from the extensive to the intensive margin. In economic magnitude, for a firm whose promised PPP amount is 10% higher than another firm, its return-IJC correlation during this period is also higher by 0.03, which is sizable given that the average correlation is 0.14.Download: Download high-res image (228KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 6. Cross-sectional evidence: Obligated Paycheck Protection Program awards and return-IJC correlations.This figure summarizes the relationship between individual stock returns-IJC correlations (y-axis) and the log of total obligated PPP awards (x-axis) from February 2020 to March 2021. For demonstration purposes, we sort firms into four groups by their obligated PPP awards: (i) Not a Covid funding recipient (log(award+1) = 0); (ii) log(award+1) from 0 to 10; (iii) log(award+1) from 10 to 15; (iv) log(award+1) above 15. The dashed lines indicate the actual 90% confidence interval.While a correlation measure is illustrative, it introduces generated regressor concerns and the literature primarily focuses on returns’ sensitivities to macro shocks. As a result, we next formalize the cross section evidence in a panel framework as follows: yti=αi+β1IJCshockt+β2IJCshockt∗Xi+ɛt,where yti is the stock returns of firm i on IJC announcement day t. Xi is a firm-level fiscal stimulus expectation proxy. αi is firm-level fixed effects. Finally, β2 is of interest. The unit of observation is firm-IJC announcement day. The time sample is identical to our time-series Covid sample (i.e., February 2020 to March 2021, excluding 03/19/2020, 03/26/2020, 04/02/2020, and other macro and monetary announcement dates). For consistency, we only consider firms in the S&P500 universe that have available all of the firm-level variables that we discuss in Section 5; we are able to keep most (90%) of the firms under this rule.16 Table 4 presents the results of a regression using Xis from our second cross section; specifically, it includes all three aforementioned funding measures in two transformations (logarithm and revenue scaling), and each column corresponds to each measure. The PPP-related measures in columns (2) and (5) are our preferred main measures. Panel A considers all IJC days and replicates the graphical evidence above in the return sensitivity space, with significant and positive interaction coefficients across all columns. Based on the log PPP range in Fig. 6, individual stock returns could increase by 33∼53 bps when a 0.1 IJC shock arrives (approximately one SD), which is economically sizable. In addition, Panel B shows that the positive relationship comes from bad IJC announcement days, with higher interaction coefficients.17 This bad-IJC-day result is also consistent with our time series evidence. In columns (4)–(6), we control for the size effect by dividing PPP and Covid award amounts by the firm’s 2019 revenue. Results are robust. To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the first efforts linking this firm-level fiscal spending and PPP data to stock market data in the literature.18Table 4. Panel mechanism test: Covid stimulus spending.5.3. Firm crisis impact measuresMore broadly speaking, investors might expect firms that are likely to be or have been more affected by Covid to receive more government support in the future. Both expected and realized impact are likely to enter active policy debates and are therefore meaningful in investors’ formations of their cash flow expectations. We use six measures to capture Covid impact to firm fundamentals in our third cross-section.First, we use a novel dataset from LinkUp that indexes all job listings directly from employer websites in real-time. Our first Covid impact measure is changes in the number of job postings from a firm’s 2019 average to its 2020 April-May average. One advantage of this measure is its foresighted nature; firms cut their job listings when they expect weaker business prospects in the near future. We also consider realized impacts in early 2020: the quarter-on-quarter growth rates of total revenue between 2019Q2 and 2020Q2 (to control for seasonality), the change in quarter-on-quarter earnings per share (basic, excluding extraordinary items) from 2019Q2 to 2020Q2, the change in the number of employees from fiscal year (FY) 2019 to fiscal year 2020, and revenue and EPS changes from FY 2019 to FY 2020.19 Data are obtained from Compustat Quarter, and we use the number of employees from 10-Ks, as employment data availability is very limited in 10-Qs.For all six of our Covid impact measures, a lower or more negative value indicates that the firm is (expected to be) more negatively impacted by Covid. We relegate detailed summary statistics to Online Appendix Table OA3 and discuss main observations below. On average, firms reduced their job listings by -39% during the initial impact of Covid. Actual employment changes from fiscal year 2019 to 2020 could include some positive labor growth, which is not surprising given that by the end of 2020 two rounds of stimulus packages had come in; this also makes fiscal-year-based measures less appealing for our test. The quarterly financial measures show a wide dispersion of changes in firm revenue and EPS, with the latter being more negatively skewed (with the 5th percentile at about -$11 and the 95th at $4). Due to the skewed nature of these financial variables, we take the percentile rank of these measures in our analysis. Table 5 shows results as consistent and robust as Table 4 using Covid impact measures based on fundamentals. Significant and negative interaction coefficients indicate that firms that experience more Covid impact also exhibit significantly higher return sensitivity to IJC shocks, hence a stronger “Main Street pain, Wall Street gain” effect. Results again come from bad IJC days.Table 5. Panel mechanism test: Covid impact on firm fundamentals.The three novel cross-sections that we construct from various data sources (bill mentioning, obligated and actual fiscal support, and Covid damage) give our research a unique opportunity to answer some interesting questions: for instance, during the height of fiscal activity from 2020 to early 2021, who gets what? Our evidence is generally consistent with Gourinchas et al. (2021), showing that the majority of industries mentioned more often in actual bills are also more affected by the initial Covid crisis. More discussions are relegated to Online Appendix Section OD.
article.6. Mechanism: Pre-covid evidence using g: In this section, we expand our cross-sectional test to a pre-Covid sample. To start, we search for the main source of fiscal spending distributed to firms and industries before 2020 in order to have enough variations to test. Specifically, we evaluate both actual dollar amounts and public attention. Fiscal spending to individual firms in the form of direct cash, forgivable loans, or subsidies (or those categorized under these emergency fund codes) is minimal during our pre-2020 sample period. This stands in sharp contrast to another type of fiscal spending that also reaches firms: government procurement contracts.To illustrate the magnitude, Fig. 7 plots rolling-12 month total fiscal spending to firms in the S&P500 universe. Fiscal spending through procurement contracts fluctuates around 300 billion dollars annually before 2020. After 2020, it only mildly increases, showing less dominance as a source of fiscal spending compared to the stimulus packages. Fig. 8 shows the monthly mentions of words related to “stimulus” and “procurement”, respectively, in Google Trends. Words associated with fiscal stimuli are scarcely mentioned until after 2020, with notable spikes aligning with the months when the CARES, CAA, and ARP Acts were signed into law. Conversely, mentions related to procurement and government contracts consistently maintain a high level of public attention, including before 2020. As a result, procurement emerges as our most viable option for examining the cross-sectional test before 2020. While Section 5 leverages one specific period in recent U.S. history marked by unprecedented and massive fiscal stimulus discussions, we next explore a more general empirical framework that allows for both longitudinal and cross-sectional variations to test our hypothesis.We test the following prediction. When FP is actively discussed nationwide amid heightened budgetary debates, firms and industries that are expected to receive more fiscal spending via procurement contracts should exhibit higher individual stock returns when bad IJC shocks occur. Empirically, we use recent procurement award amounts as a proxy for expected future fiscal dependence and our text-based variable from Section 4 to indicate periods of high FP speculation. This limits our sample to the years 2013–2019. We expand our announcement-day-level specification (Eq. (2)) into a firm-IJC announcement day panel specification: (3)yti=(β1+β3FPτ+β4FPτ∗Procurementti)∗IJCshockt+αi+γt+β2FPτ+ɛt, where yti is the log open-to-close return for firm i on announcement day t. IJCshockt is the IJC shock on announcement day and FPτ is the fiscal policy mentions during quarter τ to capture periods of the sample when FP discussions are heightened; both are used in the time-series evidence (see Table 3 and Section 4). αi and γt indicate firm and date (i.e., event date) fixed effects, respectively. We focus on bad IJC announcement days, which, according to our evidence thus far, should be where FP expectations matter.Download: Download high-res image (237KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 7. Total amount of fiscal spending to firms during the paper sample period.This figure plots total fiscal spending amounts using a 12-month rolling window. The thick solid line represents procurement obligations plus Covid-related stimulus spending; the thin dashed line represents procurement only. Both variables are in billions of dollars, and are based on the authors’ own construction and raw data from USAspending.gov. We systematically limit our firm universe to S&P 500 firms (as we focus on these in the paper). Appendix Figure OA1 shows more details with industry decomposition of the procurement line.Download: Download high-res image (643KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 8. Public attention paid to fiscal stimulus versus government procurement, according to Google Search.This figure depicts the relative public attention paid to two different fiscal spending tools at the monthly frequency from 01/2013 to 04/2021 (one month after the end of our paper sample for completeness), with the first data point under the Procurement line (green) scaled to 1. For stimulus, we use two simple terms: economic stimulus and Covid stimulus; for procurement, we use two other simple terms: procurement and government contracts. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)Table 6. Panel mechanism evidence before 2020: Measuring fiscal spending with procurement contract data.What is new is that Procurementti now measures firm i’s exposure to government procurement awards, proxied by recent past 6-month procurement award amounts. Consistent with Section 5.2, we consider both log transformations and revenue scaling. We construct Procurementti at both the firm-time level and the industry-time level.20 We also find that the magnitude of fiscal spending across and within most industries appears quite persistent over time. For instance, manufacturing and construction always rank in the top two in terms of absolute dollar amounts, while healthcare and retail receive the fewest procurement contracts (before 2020). Once scaled by revenue, similar ranks recur; for instance, the real estate and construction industries continue to dominate the fiscal dependence, and the retail industry’s fiscal dependence is pretty close to zero. Professional has a high fiscal dependence — procurement contracts account for roughly 20% of the industry’s revenue. Detailed plots are shown in Internet Appendix Figure OA1.In Eq. (3), β3 tests our main aggregate prediction, and β4 tests our main cross section prediction. Both coefficients are expected to be positive under our hypothesis. Table 6 presents the results. Column (1) is the baseline aggregate result, replicating our main aggregate results using the pre-2020 period only.21 The significant and positive coefficient estimate of β3 indicates that when FP mentions and speculations are one SD higher than average, the market return response to a 10% IJC shock increases significantly by 34 basis points. Column (2) of Table 6 expands the preceding column into a panel framework, conditional on the availability of data on open and close prices and ticker-CUSIP matching. β3 has a quite similar economic magnitude, with a smaller standard error, expected given the panel size. Columns (3) and (6) use a firm’s log past 6 m procurement amount and fiscal dependence measures in the triple interaction, respectively. The issue with a firm-level procurement proxy is that around 113 firms in the S&P500 universe have never received a procurement contract during this sample period; firm fixed effects hence absorb about 21% of the data points. However, unlike stimulus awards that are designed to give firm-specific relief, many procurement contracts are allocated to a group of bidders that come with their own supply chain network. It is plausible that payments to other firms in the same industry might generate positive earnings spillovers to a given firm indirectly, and investors observe the network. As a result, we find it difficult to interpret the coefficient estimates of β4 under columns (3) and (6).Download: Download high-res image (489KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 9. Traditional firm characteristics.We sort S&P500 firms into 5 bins based on firms’ end-of −2019 characteristics: (1) standard size and value factor (B/M, E/P); (2) free cash flow (FCF = operating cash flow (OANCF)-gross capital expenditures (CAPX)); (3) risk (leverage = (long-term debt+short-term debt)/shareholder equity). The portfolio takes the return difference between the lowest (lowest-size, lowest-B/M, lowest-E/P, lowest-FCF, lowest-leverage) and the highest quintile bins. In each portfolio, average returns can be calculated using bad IJC days (when the actual IJC number is higher/worse than expected), good IJC days (when the actual IJC number is lower/better than expected), and non-IJC days. Returns are in basis points; our sample period runs from February 2020 to March 2021, excluding 03/19, 03/26, and 04/02 of 2020 and other macro and FOMC overlaps. Robustness evidence for Fig. 5: Industry mentions in actual bills. This figure uses three other bills. See other details in Fig. 5. Acts: (A) CARES was initially introduced in the House of Representatives on January 24, 2019 as H.R. 748 (Middle Class Health Benefits Tax Repeal Act of 2019); it passed the House on July 17, 2019. It passed the Senate as the Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act on March 25, 2020, and was signed into law by President Donald Trump on March 27, 2020. (B) The HEROES Act was introduced in House of Representatives on May 12, 2020 as H.R. 6800; it passed the House on May 15, 2020. (C) The CAA was a spending bill, H.R. 133, for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2021, and was the product of weeks of intense negotiations and compromise between Democrats and Republicans; it passed Congress on December 21, 2020, and was signed into law by President Donald Trump on December 27, 2020. (d) The ARP Act was introduced in House of Representatives on January 14, 2021 as H.R. 1319; it passed the House on February 27, 2021, passed the Senate on March 6, 2021, and was signed into law by President Joe Biden on March 11, 2021. Scatter plot of monthly unemployment announcement surprises and announcement day open-to-close returns. Covid and normal periods are as defined in the main paper.To address this, we consider industry-time-level procurement support proxies in columns (4) and (7) as our main results. The number of observations is preserved. Triple interaction coefficient estimates in both columns are significant and positive. Lastly, columns (5) and (8) include date fixed effects to further control for all possible time series determinants. Our results remain robust, both statistically and economically.One interesting extension is to examine whether this result is stronger during Democratic or Republican presidencies. Our sample (2013–2019) covers the Obama presidency and the Trump presidency. Belo et al. (2013) show that during Democratic presidencies, firms with high government exposure exhibit higher cash flows and stock returns. In our context, investors should price individual stocks with greater government exposure higher, particularly during the Obama part of our procurement sample period, i.e.,2013–2016. Consistent with the literature, we find that our result is both statistically and economically stronger during the Obama presidency. Detailed results are relegated to Table A.6.
article.7. Discussion: A conceptual framework.Empirical choices in our research are consistent with the following conceptual framework. Developing a formal model that incorporates these scenarios merits future work. The baseline scenario (A) describes traditional asset pricing models, where a bad macroeconomic shock leads to changes in state variables such as volatility, risk aversion, and expected growth, resulting in lower stock prices. Conversely, a good macro shock would lead to higher stock prices under the same framework. In a non-ZLB scenario (B) with active policy expectations, when a bad macro shock arrives, investors expect MP to be more lax in the future, resulting in a counteracting force that drives up the asset prices. This MP expectation mechanism operates through discount rate channels (i.e., interest rate and risk premium channels) and has been discussed in existing literature. To keep it simple yet realistic (with respect to recent decades), we assume that fiscal policy is mostly an expansionary policy tool, and the primary means of FP are the tax and spending policy tools, which should directly change expected cash flow growth. Scenario (C), a ZLB scenario with active policy expectations, is the most analogous to the sample we study in this paper (i.e., a low interest rate environment). The main difference with (B) is that MP is constrained at the ZLB. In this context, when bad shocks occur, public attention to and expectations about FP tools could increase.Download: Download high-res image (346KB)Download: Download full-size imageIn general, this conceptual framework underpins our empirical decisions to separately examine CF and DR channels throughout the paper and to distinguish between bad and good IJC day responses. Additionally, this mechanism should exist without the Covid period, which is tested in both the aggregate and cross section parts of the paper.Traditional cash flow sensitive portfolios.We form portfolios based on several reported firm characteristics and risk proxies as of the end of 2019; of these, some characteristics have been shown in the literature to be associated with cash flow sensitivity. The portfolio takes the return difference between the lowest and the highest quintile bins; within each quintile, value-weighted average returns can be calculated on bad, good, and non-IJC days.The first four blue solid bars in Fig. 9 demonstrate that firms with high sensitivity to market cash flow news (i.e., small size, high B/M, high E/P, low free cash flow) outperform when IJC numbers are worse than expected, that is, according to our mechanism, when more expansionary fiscal policy is expected. Such highly cash flow sensitive firms perform worse on good IJC days (red shaded bars) or non-announcement days (hollow bars) than on bad IJC days. The bar magnitude on non-bad IJC days is statistically and significantly smaller than on bad IJC days, which further supports the mechanism established in the main analysis. We also sort firms based on their leverage or riskiness conditions at the end of 2019, where leverage is defined as (long-term debt+short-term debt) divided by shareholder equity. We find that the low-minus-high leverage portfolio shows significant and positive returns on good IJC days, which is consistent with the monetary policy expectation channel that we document above. When good IJC news arrives, investors may expect monetary policy to tighten, which would be proportionally worse news for highly leveraged firms. We also find that the low-minus-high leverage portfolio shows close to zero and insignificant returns on both bad and non-IJC days, which indicates that leverage or riskiness is not the channel that creates the paper’s headline phenomenon.Monthly macro announcements.We use monthly announcements of unemployment rates and non-farm payrolls, two other often-studied labor condition variables (see Fisher et al., 2022), to test whether stock returns exhibit significantly different responses to labor news surprises before and after 2020. As shown in Fig. A.3, stock returns during the Covid period increase with bad unemployment rate news, holding a correlation at 0.793 (p-value = 0.000), which is statistically higher than the correlation during the normal period, 0.035 (p-value = 0.803). We conduct a separate analysis using 7 monthly macro announcements in Online Appendix Section OE. The fact that not all macro announcements show similar patterns – for instance, inflation does not – provides further support to a cash-flow-related mechanism.
article.8. Conclusion: Our paper starts with establishing a few stylized facts. Compared to earlier years, in 2020–2021 there are significantly heightened fiscal policy mentions when bad IJC news comes out, and stock returns increase with IJC shocks. In addition, the IJC effect mostly influences stock returns through the expected cash flow channel and is pronounced during bad IJC days. Given these stylized facts, we hypothesize a specific cash flow mechanism that can be generalized, and provide empirical evidence both in time series and cross section. Time-varying fiscal policy expectations, gauged by a news-based topic mention measure, significantly explain the time variation in return responses to IJC shocks on IJC announcement days. Next, we show two key pieces of evidence in the cross section. In general, firms that are expected to receive more fiscal spending – proxied by Covid stimulus after 2020 or procurement awards before 2020 – exhibit higher individual stock returns when bad IJC shocks appear. Our paper mainly contributes to the literature by empirically establishing a fiscal policy expectations state variable, and we use labor news announcements as an identification strategy to help “sign” the mechanism. Future research should examine the macroeconomic effects and welfare effects of fiscal policy expectations. The fact that people have fiscal policy expectations when bad news arrives could feed back to the macro economy through consumption behaviors, labor options, and borrowing decisions, as we observe in the inflation hikes and the Great Resignation of late 2021 and 2022. In addition, while the Covid crisis triggered an unprecedented adverse shock to the labor market, the capital market gain due to investor anticipation of fiscal stimuli is not trivial in dollar terms. From February 2020 to March 2021, the average daily capital gain in the S&P 500 market is 73 billion dollars on bad IJC days, 18 billion dollars on good IJC days, and 44 billion dollars on non-IJC days (see details in Online Appendix Table OA4). Overall, our work implies that the distributional effect of fiscal policy could also transmit through the investor expectations channel, which gets capitalized into the stock market at a high frequency.
